kept.text:
This guy with no design background just built a 3D website that would have cost a client $8,000 at any agency.

He didn't hire a developer. He didn't open Figma. He opened Claude and started typing what he wanted to see. 3D objects that move on scroll. Animations that follow the cursor. Cinematic transitions between sections.

Claude generated the code. Emergent rendered it live. He adjusted with more prompts until the screen showed something he couldn't have built in 6 months of learning.

The whole thing took one afternoon.

What used to require a senior developer and a $15,000 budget now requires knowing how to describe what you want clearly.

That's it. That's the only skill.

Most people have Claude. Almost none of them know how to actually use it.

This article below fixes that.

quoted_tweet.text:
The Chegg story might be the biggest AI casualty of any company so far.

Chegg was worth $14.5 billion in February 2021. ChatGPT launched 21 months later. Today Chegg is worth $114 million.

The speed matters. On May 2, 2023, five months after ChatGPT launched, CEO Dan Rosensweig admitted on an earnings call that ChatGPT was hurting customer growth. The stock fell 48% in a single day. It was the first publicly listed company in history to formally acknowledge AI-induced revenue damage. What followed was not a slow recovery. It was a second collapse.

Chegg's business model was a decade-long moat: a database of millions of pre-solved homework problems, indexed by Google, monetized at $19.95 per month. ChatGPT broke every link simultaneously. It was free. It explained concepts instead of just delivering answers. It accepted unlimited follow-up questions. The moat did not leak. It evaporated.

Then Google finished the job. When AI Overviews rolled out across search in 2024 and 2025, Google pulled answers directly from Chegg's own content library into search results, eliminating the click-through that was Chegg's primary acquisition channel. Search traffic dropped 60%. Chegg filed an antitrust lawsuit against Google in 2025. The traffic had already gone.

By Q1 2025, subscribers had dropped 31% year-on-year to 3.2 million. Quarterly revenue in February 2026 was $72.7 million. That is a 49% year-over-year decline. 1,396 employees laid off across four rounds since June 2024. The stock now trades at $1.00, one cent above the NYSE delisting threshold.

The company that sold answers for $19.95 a month was killed by a product that gave better answers for free.

kept.text:
Here's how to get pixel perfect screens from Figma with clean maintainable code using codex.

You need to use the atomic design paradigm.

It forces every component to own its logic. Debugging gets a lot easier after that. LLMs tend to create fewer bugs when the repo follows atomic design.

If you repo does not have proper components, you should just use a existing system like shadcn or mantine.

For Figma
Don’t treat the screen as one thing.
Break it into layout + components. Then prompt each part separately.

In a mono frontend/backend repo, I get almost pixel perfect UI with barely any bugs. Everything just connects properly. 

For bigger, more complex screens
Go one level deeper.

Start with the layout. Break down every component. Each component gets its own thread.
You get way more control this way. Things stop randomly breaking, and when they do break, you know exactly where to look.

Use this with git worktrees, and multiple threads for multiple component.

profile_bio.description: Design ✦ Code ✦ Bugs

kept.text:
We're reshaping how we work with Startup founders in Adria Studio

The old workflow used to look a lot like this:

Design in Figma
Wait for Feedback
Design another iteration in Figma
Get it Approved
Wait for their Eng to implement it (1 day)
Live version doesn't feel right (can't tweak on-spot)
Do revisions (1-2 days)
Get it approved (1-2 days)
Wait for eng to implement again (1 day)

The new workflow looks a lot like:

Design and implement (1-2 days)
Configure the versions live with the client (1 hour)
Get it approved (usually instant, or within 1-2 days)

This allows you to figure out what *feels* right in a matter of hours, versus it taking days prior.

kept.text:
Figma n'est pas mort. Ça va être le plus gros comeback de la décennie.

Pourquoi ?

Les modèles sont une commodité. Claude est un highliter de nouveaux use cases AI, sans focus. Figma version IA sera 1000 fois meilleur que Claude Design sur le métier de designer, parce que Figma fait QUE ça depuis 10 ans.

Le pattern "X a tué Y" est en train de devenir le plus gros test de QI de la décennie. Il révèle deux confusions :

1. Ce qu'est un software. Un produit, c'est pas un modèle. C'est 10 ans de workflow, de data, de network effects, d'intégrations, de muscle memory de millions d'users. Le modèle, c'est 5% du stack.

2. Ce qu'est un métier. Les tâches changent. Les métiers restent. Le designer de 2030 utilisera des outils qu'on imagine pas, comme le designer de 2010 a abandonné Photoshop pour Figma. Il sera toujours designer.

Ceux qui confondent outil et métier vont passer la décennie à crier au loup à chaque release.

quoted_tweet.text:
Claude Design a tué les Designers !

Anthropic a lancé Claude Design.

Figma : -85 % en bourse en 7 mois.

À chaque cycle, la même prophétie.
À chaque cycle, la même règle.

1990 : Photoshop tue les photographes.
Il y a plus de photographes professionnels aujourd'hui qu'en 1990. Les médiocres ont disparu.

1995 : le web tue l'imprimerie.
Ceux qui ont migré vers le packaging et le grand format sont là, les autres ne sont plus.

2022 : ChatGPT tue les rédacteurs.
Ceux avec une voix sont devenus rares et mieux payés. Les interchangeables ont disparu.

2024 : Claude Code tue les développeurs.
Les bons codent 10 fois plus vite. Les copieurs Stack Overflow ont disparu.

2026 : Claude Design tue les designers.
La règle ne changera pas.

L'outil tue la médiocrité. Jamais le métier.

kept.text:
Just had a retro on a feature with one of my client front-end teams, where we disagreed.

They think prototyping should only happen when you genuinely need to discover something. Otherwise figma first, always.

I think the speed of AI-enabled prototyping has challenged this.

In figma I'd do one or two variations of an interaction and ship the one that worked the best. With AI I can prototype five or ten variations in much less time, experience them, and pick the right one.

That's the whole argument. More options explored in less time means a better final product. The figma file stops being the source of truth, but the product gets better.

Drag-and-drop, animation, edge cases, the way two states meet. You can't sketch your way to those. You have to feel them. And now you can feel ten of them instead of one.

We're running this at @halaska right now. Don't fully know the answer yet. But I'm pretty sure the studios that figure it out first will ship product the figma-only studios can't catch.

profile_bio.description: Founder Orenji Studio | Design Engineer. We design cool stuff ✨ → https://t.co/b2CkhEXtQs

kept.text:
Claude Design only works if you know how to prompt it.

I've tested every AI design tool shipped this year. Claude Design is the only one I still open.

Here's the workflow I use to ship production UI in hours:

1/ Start with a design system
> Upload a Figma file with your fonts, colors, spacing, components
> Tell Claude to match it exactly
> Every screen after that stays on brand
> This one step separates production quality from generic container soup

2/ Prompt for an entire flow, not one screen
> Ask for the whole thing at once
> Projects, Tasks, Settings, Search all together
> Claude Design handles context across screens
> You save hours

3/ Layer features iteratively
> First prompt: core screens
> Second prompt: dark mode, dropdowns, search
> Third prompt: command K, filters
> Build features in rounds, keep each prompt focused

4/ Be brutally specific
> "Add command K search with fuzzy filtering" beats "add search"
> Name the interaction, name the component
> The more constraints you give, the closer it ships to production

5/ Spin up a branded deck from the same system
> Use the same design system to generate a product showcase
> One more prompt, case study format
> Export as PDF, done

Design systems used to be a roadmap item.
Now they're the seed that grows your entire product.
Build once. Reuse forever.

kept.text:
